questions.text: I feel that a reliable person is a valuable person.
I would rather be exhausted from helping than rested from self-care.
I am rarely at a neutral emotional baseline — something is always registering strongly.
I notice when someone asks a question they already know the answer to in order to make a point.
I hold the view that moral attention to suffering — the active decision to let it matter — is a habit that strengthens with practice.
I am someone who defines the mission rather than inheriting it.
I take pride in being the driving force behind a project's success.
I believe that treating others' suffering as morally binding is not a philosophical position but a lived practice that I try to maintain daily.
I believe that approaching emotional situations with structured reasoning produces outcomes that are fairer to everyone involved.
I feel that the wellbeing of those around me is ultimately more important than my own wellbeing.
I am highly aware of the social dynamics playing out in digital communication, not just in-person.
I routinely structure my life to maximize personal freedom and opportunity.
I find that social hierarchies are mostly invisible to me unless someone explains them.
I hold the view that discipline is freedom, because it eliminates the chaos of indecision.
I usually adjust my stance to keep the group aligned.
I believe that staying loose and responsive compounds into a richer range of experiences.
I hold the view that a steady emotional baseline is simply the way I experience the world.
I can feel adrenaline surge through me in response to relatively minor provocations.
